Client 1: Roughly… I’d say maybe seventy-something thousand a year? It kind of drips in.
Client 2: It’s closer to eighty. About eighty thousand two hundred a year, if we’re using the annual number.
Advisor: Got it—about $80,200 a year total household gross coming in. And when you say it “drips in,” is that like dividends and interest hitting quarterly?
Client 1: Yes, quarterly. I always forget which months, but it’s not every month.
Client 2: Right—quarterly interest and dividends. It’s mostly in Margaret’s name, not mine.

Client 1: Um… maybe three thousand? Two-eight? It depends on the month.
Client 2: It’s not three. It’s about twenty-seven hundred a month, unless we have a big one-off.
Advisor: About $2,700 per month for ongoing expenses—thank you. When you say “all-in,” does that include property insurance and those kinds of bills too?
Client 1: Yes, I lump it all together. Groceries, utilities, insurance—everything we pay regularly.
Advisor: Great. And do you have any monthly debt payments—mortgage, car loan, credit cards you’re carrying a balance on?

Client 1: Roughly… I’d say maybe seventy-something thousand a year? It kind of drips in.
Client 2: It’s closer to eighty. About eighty thousand two hundred a year, if we’re using the annual number.
Advisor: Got it—about $80,200 a year total household gross coming in. And when you say it “drips in,” is that like dividends and interest hitting quarterly?
Client 1: Yes, quarterly. I always forget which months, but it’s not every month.
Client 2: Right—quarterly interest and dividends. It’s mostly in Margaret’s name, not mine.

Client 2: Exactly. Keep the house separate.
Client 1: Okay, that makes sense.
Advisor: So adding up those investable accounts—taxable about $169,050, retirement about $150,500, bank about $35,050, private markets about $7,800—does that sound right as your investable total, around $362,400?
Client 2: Yes, $362,400 is right.
Client 1: Yes—around three-sixty-two thousand.

Client 1: Yes.
Advisor: And your home—just so we have the full picture—what do you estimate the primary residence is worth today?
Client 2: Around three-fifty-four, five. $354,500 is the number we’ve been using.
Client 1: And no loan on it. We paid it off years ago.
Advisor: Great—home value about $354,500, and no mortgage outstanding. Let me pause—when I say “investable assets,” I mean the accounts we can invest from: taxable, retirement, bank/cash, and that private markets piece. Not the house. Does that match how you think about it?

Advisor: Hi Margaret, hi Josep—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time today. Before we get into the weeds, what would make this meeting feel useful for you?
Client 1: Thanks. Honestly, I just want to know we’re okay month to month and not doing anything… dumb.
Client 2: And I want the numbers to line up. Income, taxes, where the accounts actually are—clean and simple.

Client 1: Yes, we’re in the US.
Client 2: Yep, US.
Advisor: Okay. And marital status—are you two married, or is it a domestic partnership situation?
Client 2: Married.
Client 1: Married, yes.

Client 1: Roughly… I’d say maybe seventy-something thousand a year? It kind of drips in.
Client 2: It’s closer to eighty. About eighty thousand two hundred a year, if we’re using the annual number.
Advisor: Got it—about $80,200 a year total household gross coming in. And when you say it “drips in,” is that like dividends and interest hitting quarterly?
Client 1: Yes, quarterly. I always forget which months, but it’s not every month.
Client 2: Right—quarterly interest and dividends. It’s mostly in Margaret’s name, not mine.

Client 2: Zero dependents.
Advisor: Perfect. Now, risk—this is where people hear different things. When I say “risk tolerance,” I mean how much market up-and-down you can live with and still stick to the plan.
Client 1: I’m… moderate, I think. I don’t like big drops, but we can handle some movement.
Client 2: Moderate, yes. Not aggressive.
Advisor: Okay, moderate. Margaret, when you say “big drops,” are you thinking like your monthly spending dropping, or the account values dropping?

Client 2: Yes—Margaret is Client 1, I’m Client 2.
Client 1: Yes, that’s right.
Advisor: Thanks. And just to place us in context—this is a US RIA relationship, meaning you’re working with me here in the U.S. advisory setup. Does that sound right to you, or am I mis-stating it?
Client 2: No, that’s right—U.S. RIA.
Client 1: Yes, we’re here in the U.S., with you.

Advisor: Hi Margaret, hi Josep—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time today. Before we get into the weeds, what would make this meeting feel useful for you?
Client 1: Thanks. Honestly, I just want to know we’re okay month to month and not doing anything… dumb.
Client 2: And I want the numbers to line up. Income, taxes, where the accounts actually are—clean and simple.
Advisor: Perfect. We’ll keep it plain-English and we’ll keep it accurate. Margaret, can you give me the quick version of what money comes in during the year—roughly?
Client 1: Roughly… I’d say maybe seventy-something thousand a year? It kind of drips in.

Client 2: No, that’s gross on the statements. The tax is separate.
Client 1: Right—sorry, yes, gross. Before taxes.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you—gross before taxes. And you’re in about the 32% tax bracket these days, is that what you’ve been seeing on your returns?
Client 2: Yes, 32%.
Client 1: Unfortunately, yes—thirty-two percent.

Advisor: Okay—about $129,100 of coverage, costing about $50 a month, and it’s set to run until 2039. We can review whether it still makes sense given you’re both retired.
Client 2: Yes, please.
Advisor: Quick check on liabilities again—besides the credit cards you pay off, any other loans at all? Personal loan, line of credit, anything like that?
Client 2: No.
Client 1: No, none.

Client 2: It’s quarterly. She’s right that it gets moved around, but the source pays quarterly.
Advisor: Got it—quarterly from the source, then you two smooth it out in checking. Thanks.
Advisor: And just so I’m not mixing whose is whose—Margaret, your gross annual income is about $55,900, and Josep yours is about $24,300. Is that still the right split?
Client 1: Yes, mine’s about fifty-six thousand gross.
Client 2: And mine’s about twenty-four thousand, yes.

Advisor: Fair point—understand first, then decide. Margaret, do you remember what the benefit amount is on that disability policy?
Client 1: I think it’s about one-twenty-nine thousand? Something like that.
